text:
Use `-X POST` with `-H` to set the content type and `-d` for the body:

```bash
curl -X POST https://api.example.com/endpoint \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name": "Ada", "role": "admin"}'
```

A few practical variations:

**Body from a file** (keeps shell quoting out of the way):
```bash
curl -X POST https://api.example.com/endpoint \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d @payload.json
```

**With an auth header**, which most real endpoints need:
```bash
curl -X POST https://api.example.com/endpoint \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -d '{"name": "Ada"}'
```

**See the response status and headers** while debugging — add `-i` (include response headers) or `-v` (verbose, shows the full request/response exchange):
```bash
curl -i -X POST https://api.example.com/endpoint \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name": "Ada"}'
```

Notes worth knowing:
- `-d` implies a POST, so `-X POST` is technically redundant when you use `-d` — but keeping it explicit is clearer.
- `-d` sends `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded` by default, so the `-H "Content-Type: application/json"` is what actually makes it a JSON request. Don't omit it.
- Use single quotes around the JSON so the shell doesn't try to expand `$` or mangle the double quotes inside.

promptText: How do I POST a JSON body to an endpoint with curl?
